Deep time to the present day

A short history of snakes.

From limbed lizard ancestors to the mythologies and medicines they inspired, snakes have been shaping — and being shaped by — the world for well over 100 million years.

~150–170 million years ago

Evolution from lizards

Snakes descend from lizards that gradually lost their limbs, likely while adapting to burrowing or aquatic lifestyles. Fossils like Najash rionegrina still show tiny hind-leg remnants, a clue to this transition.

~100 million years ago

Diversification begins

After the limb loss, snakes began radiating into new ecological niches — burrowers, swimmers, and climbers — setting the stage for the roughly 3,900 species alive today.

Ancient civilizations

Mythology and symbolism

Snakes shed their skin and appeared to be reborn, making them powerful symbols of healing, fertility, and transformation — from the Egyptian uraeus and the Rod of Asclepius to the feathered serpent Quetzalcoatl and the World Serpent of Norse myth.

1881

The first antivenom

French scientist Albert Calmette developed the first snake antivenom in 1895 by injecting horses with small amounts of cobra venom and harvesting the resulting antibodies — a method still at the core of antivenom production today.

20th century

Modern herpetology

Advances in taxonomy, venom biochemistry, and field research transformed snakes from folklore subjects into a rigorously studied group — reshaping medicine along the way, since snake venom proteins have informed treatments for blood pressure and blood clotting disorders.

Today

Conservation in the spotlight

Habitat loss, the illegal pet trade, and persecution now threaten hundreds of snake species. Conservation programs increasingly pair captive breeding and habitat protection with public education to change how people see snakes.

Glossary

  • Venomous: Injects venom through fangs.
  • Constriction: Killing prey by coiling.
  • Elapid: Fixed front-fanged snakes.
  • Viper: Hinged long-fanged venomous snake.
